summary:
Iron working in China began around the 6th–5th centuries BC and developed along a different technological trajectory than in the West — Chinese metallurgists developed cast iron (iron heated to full liquidity and poured into molds) far earlier than anywhere else in the world, around 500 BC, a technology not achieved in Europe until the 14th century AD. This was possible because Chinese blast furnace technology could achieve temperatures high enough to melt iron (over 1,150°C), whereas Western bloomery furnaces produced only wrought iron.

The early adoption of cast iron in China had major agricultural and military consequences: cast iron plows, hoes, and other tools transformed agricultural productivity, enabling the intensive farming of the Yangzi delta and other fertile areas that supported the growing populations of the Warring States period. Cast iron weapons were initially more brittle than wrought iron and bronze, but techniques for annealing (controlled cooling to reduce brittleness) were developed by the Han dynasty. The [[event:1220|Chinese Iron Casting]] details specific Qin-Han developments. China's early mastery of high-temperature metallurgy also contributed to later innovations including steel production techniques that preceded Western equivalents by over a millennium.
